chunk_summary: This section discusses variations in screening metrics for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in India, emphasizing the use of MUAC and WHZ/WLZ, documentation strategies, and risk-based care to address discrepancies in clinical and programmatic criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ as screening tools for MAM in India.
Learn the documentation strategy for managing discrepancies in MAM screening metrics.
Recognize the importance of risk-based care and follow-up intensity in MAM management.
Appreciate the role of caregiver education in ensuring sustained recovery and preventing relapse.
key_facts: MUAC and WHZ/WLZ are both recommended by WHO for identifying moderate wasting, but Indian programs often prioritize WHZ plus oedema.
The Ministry of Women and Child Development (MoWCD) reserves MUAC for emergencies like pandemics or natural disasters.
Discrepancies between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ can result in children being excluded from local entitlement pathways.
Clinical strategy recommends recording both MUAC and WHZ/WLZ, using the more severe measure for risk stratification.
Caregivers should receive written documentation of both measures to ensure continuity of care.
Treatment goals focus on sustained recovery, clinical stability, and prevention of relapse rather than single metric thresholds.
reference_quote: The Ministry of Women and Child Development (MoWCD) has not endorsed routine use of MUAC for community screening in non-emergency settings, reserving it for emergencies such as pandemics or natural disasters.
document_key_topics: Screening metric variations for MAM in India
Risk-based care and follow-up in MAM
